'''Nippon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a [[Nippon Steel]] [[steelworks]]. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the Yahata Works, Oita Works, and Hikari Titanium Division.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – Tobata-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – Oita City, Oita Prefecture, Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:Yahata Former Main Office.JPG|thumb|right|Former Main Office, a World Heritage Site]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on [[National Route 199]]]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the Naimin Port]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the [[Government-operated Yahata Steel Works|'''Government-operated Yahata Steel Works''']] (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, [[Nippon Steel]] and [[Sumitomo Metal Industries]], one of the "Big Three" of the [[Sumitomo Group]], merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." [[Mainichi Shimbun]] (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the [[Onga River Pump Room]] (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the [[Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining]] World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
=== Overview === [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in [[1901]].]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated [[steelworks]] that began operations in [[1901]] during the [[Meiji era]]. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the [[Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks]], which had been operating since [[1887]] (Meiji 20). Before [[World War II]], it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In [[1934]] (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish [[Nippon Steel]] (Nittetsu). After World War II, in [[1950]] (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of [[Yawata Iron & Steel]]. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and [[Fuji Iron & Steel]] into [[Nippon Steel Corporation]], the works became part of the new company.
As of 2012 (before the integration with Kokura Steelworks), crude steel production at Yahata was 3.55 million tons per year, while Kokura produced 1.24 million tons annually.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|author= |date= |url=http://www.nssmc.com/ir/pdf/nssmc_jp_ar_2013_08.pdf |title=Network of Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Corporation|format=PDF |work= |publisher= |accessdate=2017-02-22 }}</ref> The total site area is 11.12 million square meters (as of March 31, 2016).<ref>[https://www.nipponsteel.com/works/yawata/about/outline.html Nippon Steel Yahata Works Overview]</ref> As of March 31, 2019, the number of employees is 3,548.<ref name="saihen"></ref>
=== Facilities and Products === [[File:Yawata Works Tobata.JPG|thumb|right|Distant view of Tobata District, Yahata Works]] The Yahata District is divided into the Tobata District in Tobata-ku, [[Kitakyushu]] (formerly Tobata Site), Yahata District in Yahatahigashi-ku (formerly Yahata Site), and the Kokura District in Kokurakita-ku (formerly Kokura Steelworks). Major facilities and the General Center (former Main Office) are located in the Tobata District.
Tobata and Yahata are connected by a dedicated 6-kilometer [[railway]] line ([[Kurogane Line]]).

On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞}} (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|ja|明治日本の産業革命遺産 製鉄・製鋼、造船、石炭産業}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
=== Overview === [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|ja|1887年}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|ja|1934年}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|ja|富士製鐵}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵}}, the works became part of the new company.
...

'''Nippon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鉄}} {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}}. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – {{ill|Tobata-ku|ja|戸畑区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu City|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – {{ill|Kokurakita-ku|ja|小倉北区}}, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – {{ill|Hikari City|ja|光市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – {{ill|Oita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a {{ill|World Heritage Site|ja|世界遺産}}]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on {{ill|National Route 199|ja|国道199号}}]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the {{ill|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works|ja|官営八幡製鐵所}} (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵}} and {{ill|Sumitomo Metal Industries|ja|住友金属工業}}, one of the "Big Three" of the {{ill|Sumitomo Group|ja|住友グループ}}, merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞}} (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|ja|明治日本の産業革命遺産 製鉄・製鋼、造船、石炭産業}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
=== Overview === [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|ja|1887年}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|ja|1934年}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|ja|富士製鐵}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵}}, the works became part of the new company.

The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – {{ill|Tobata-ku|ja|戸畑区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu City|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – {{ill|Kokurakita-ku|ja|小倉北区}}, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – {{ill|Hikari City|ja|光市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – {{ill|Oita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a {{ill|World Heritage Site|ja|世界遺産}}]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on {{ill|National Route 199|ja|国道199号}}]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the {{ill|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works|ja|官営八幡製鐵所}} (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵}} and {{ill|Sumitomo Metal Industries|ja|住友金属工業}}, one of the "Big Three" of the {{ill|Sumitomo Group|ja|住友グループ}}, merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞}} (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|ja|明治日本の産業革命遺産 製鉄・製鋼、造船、石炭産業}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
=== Overview === [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|ja|1887年}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|ja|1934年}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|ja|富士製鐵}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵}}, the works became part of the new company.
...

It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:

revision.text:
'''Nippon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鉄}} {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}}. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – {{ill|Tobata-ku|ja|戸畑区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu City|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – {{ill|Kokurakita-ku|ja|小倉北区}}, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – {{ill|Hikari City|ja|光市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – {{ill|Oita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a {{ill|World Heritage Site|ja|世界遺産}}]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on {{ill|National Route 199|ja|国道199号}}]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the {{ill|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works|ja|官営八幡製鐵所}} (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵}} and {{ill|Sumitomo Metal Industries|ja|住友金属工業}}, one of the "Big Three" of the {{ill|Sumitomo Group|ja|住友グループ}}, merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞}} (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|ja|明治日本の産業革命遺産 製鉄・製鋼、造船、石炭産業}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
=== Overview === [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|ja|1887年}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|ja|1934年}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|ja|富士製鐵}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵}}, the works became part of the new company.
...

The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – {{ill|Tobata-ku|ja|戸畑区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu City|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – {{ill|Kokurakita-ku|ja|小倉北区}}, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – {{ill|Hikari City|ja|光市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – {{ill|Oita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a {{ill|World Heritage Site|ja|世界遺産}}]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on {{ill|National Route 199|ja|国道199号}}]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the {{ill|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works|ja|官営八幡製鐵所}} (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵}} and {{ill|Sumitomo Metal Industries|ja|住友金属工業}}, one of the "Big Three" of the {{ill|Sumitomo Group|ja|住友グループ}}, merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞}} (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|ja|明治日本の産業革命遺産 製鉄・製鋼、造船、石炭産業}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
=== Overview === [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|ja|1887年}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|ja|1934年}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|ja|富士製鐵}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵}}, the works became part of the new company.
...

After World War II, in {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|ja|富士製鐵}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵}}, the works became part of the new company.
...

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Kk]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Nippon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鉄}} {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}}. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – {{ill|Tobata-ku|ja|戸畑区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu City|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – {{ill|Kokurakita-ku|ja|小倉北区}}, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – {{ill|Hikari City|ja|光市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – {{ill|Oita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a {{ill|World Heritage Site|ja|世界遺産}}]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on {{ill|National Route 199|ja|国道199号}}]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the {{ill|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works|ja|官営八幡製鐵所}} (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵}} and {{ill|Sumitomo Metal Industries|ja|住友金属工業}}, one of the "Big Three" of the {{ill|Sumitomo Group|ja|住友グループ}}, merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞}} (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|ja|明治日本の産業革命遺産 製鉄・製鋼、造船、石炭産業}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
=== Overview === [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|ja|1887年}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|ja|1934年}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|ja|富士製鐵}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵}}, the works became part of the new company.
...

It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – {{ill|Tobata-ku|ja|戸畑区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu City|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – {{ill|Kokurakita-ku|ja|小倉北区}}, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – {{ill|Hikari City|ja|光市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – {{ill|Oita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a {{ill|World Heritage Site|ja|世界遺産}}]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on {{ill|National Route 199|ja|国道199号}}]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the {{ill|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works|ja|官営八幡製鐵所}} (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵}} and {{ill|Sumitomo Metal Industries|ja|住友金属工業}}, one of the "Big Three" of the {{ill|Sumitomo Group|ja|住友グループ}}, merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞}} (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|ja|明治日本の産業革命遺産 製鉄・製鋼、造船、石炭産業}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
=== Overview === [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|ja|1887年}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|ja|1934年}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|ja|富士製鐵}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵}}, the works became part of the new company.
...

On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞}} (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|ja|明治日本の産業革命遺産 製鉄・製鋼、造船、石炭産業}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
=== Overview === [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|ja|1887年}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|ja|1934年}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|ja|富士製鐵}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵}}, the works became part of the new company.
...

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Dardar]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Nippon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鉄}} {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}}. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – {{ill|Tobata-ku|ja|戸畑区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu City|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – {{ill|Kokurakita-ku|ja|小倉北区}}, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – {{ill|Hikari City|ja|光市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – {{ill|Oita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a {{ill|World Heritage Site|ja|世界遺産}}]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on {{ill|National Route 199|ja|国道199号}}]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the {{ill|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works|ja|官営八幡製鐵所}} (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵}} and {{ill|Sumitomo Metal Industries|ja|住友金属工業}}, one of the "Big Three" of the {{ill|Sumitomo Group|ja|住友グループ}}, merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞}} (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|ja|明治日本の産業革命遺産 製鉄・製鋼、造船、石炭産業}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
=== Overview === [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|ja|1887年}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|ja|1934年}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|ja|富士製鐵}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵}}, the works became part of the new company.

revision.text:
'''Nippon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鉄}} {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}}. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – {{ill|Tobata-ku|ja|戸畑区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu City|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – {{ill|Kokurakita-ku|ja|小倉北区}}, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – {{ill|Hikari City|ja|光市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – {{ill|Oita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a {{ill|World Heritage Site|ja|世界遺産}}]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on {{ill|National Route 199|ja|国道199号}}]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the {{ill|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works|ja|官営八幡製鐵所}} (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵}} and {{ill|Sumitomo Metal Industries|ja|住友金属工業}}, one of the "Big Three" of the {{ill|Sumitomo Group|ja|住友グループ}}, merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞}} (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|ja|明治日本の産業革命遺産 製鉄・製鋼、造船、石炭産業}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
=== Overview === [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|ja|1887年}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|ja|1934年}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|ja|富士製鐵}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵}}, the works became part of the new company.
...

The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – {{ill|Tobata-ku|ja|戸畑区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu City|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – {{ill|Kokurakita-ku|ja|小倉北区}}, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – {{ill|Hikari City|ja|光市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – {{ill|Oita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a {{ill|World Heritage Site|ja|世界遺産}}]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on {{ill|National Route 199|ja|国道199号}}]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the {{ill|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works|ja|官営八幡製鐵所}} (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵}} and {{ill|Sumitomo Metal Industries|ja|住友金属工業}}, one of the "Big Three" of the {{ill|Sumitomo Group|ja|住友グループ}}, merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞}} (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|ja|明治日本の産業革命遺産 製鉄・製鋼、造船、石炭産業}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
=== Overview === [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|ja|1887年}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|ja|1934年}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|ja|富士製鐵}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵}}, the works became part of the new company.
...

'''Nippon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鉄}} {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}}. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – {{ill|Tobata-ku|ja|戸畑区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu City|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – {{ill|Kokurakita-ku|ja|小倉北区}}, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – {{ill|Hikari City|ja|光市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – {{ill|Oita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434

Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|ja|明治日本の産業革命遺産 製鉄・製鋼、造船、石炭産業}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).

"Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞}} (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>

=== Overview === [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|ja|1887年}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|ja|1934年}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|ja|富士製鐵}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵}}, the works became part of the new company.

revision.text:
'''Nippon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鉄}} {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}}. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – {{ill|Tobata-ku|ja|戸畑区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu City|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – {{ill|Kokurakita-ku|ja|小倉北区}}, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – {{ill|Hikari City|ja|光市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – {{ill|Oita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a {{ill|World Heritage Site|ja|世界遺産}}]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on {{ill|National Route 199|ja|国道199号}}]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the {{ill|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works|ja|官営八幡製鐵所}} (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵}} and {{ill|Sumitomo Metal Industries|ja|住友金属工業}}, one of the "Big Three" of the {{ill|Sumitomo Group|ja|住友グループ}}, merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞}} (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|ja|明治日本の産業革命遺産 製鉄・製鋼、造船、石炭産業}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
=== Overview === [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|ja|1887年}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|ja|1934年}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|ja|富士製鐵}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵}}, the works became part of the new company.

'''Nippon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鉄}} {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}}. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – {{ill|Tobata-ku|ja|戸畑区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu City|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – {{ill|Kokurakita-ku|ja|小倉北区}}, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – {{ill|Hikari City|ja|光市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – {{ill|Oita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a {{ill|World Heritage Site|ja|世界遺産}}]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on {{ill|National Route 199|ja|国道199号}}]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the {{ill|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works|ja|官営八幡製鐵所}} (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵}} and {{ill|Sumitomo Metal Industries|ja|住友金属工業}}, one of the "Big Three" of the {{ill|Sumitomo Group|ja|住友グループ}}, merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞}} (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|ja|明治日本の産業革命遺産 製鉄・製鋼、造船、石炭産業}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
=== Overview === [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|ja|1887年}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|ja|1934年}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|ja|富士製鐵}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵}}, the works became part of the new company.
...

The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – {{ill|Tobata-ku|ja|戸畑区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu City|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – {{ill|Kokurakita-ku|ja|小倉北区}}, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – {{ill|Hikari City|ja|光市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – {{ill|Oita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a {{ill|World Heritage Site|ja|世界遺産}}]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on {{ill|National Route 199|ja|国道199号}}]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the {{ill|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works|ja|官営八幡製鐵所}} (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵}} and {{ill|Sumitomo Metal Industries|ja|住友金属工業}}, one of the "Big Three" of the {{ill|Sumitomo Group|ja|住友グループ}}, merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞}} (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|ja|明治日本の産業革命遺産 製鉄・製鋼、造船、石炭産業}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
=== Overview === [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|ja|1887年}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|ja|1934年}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|ja|富士製鐵}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵}}, the works became part of the new company.
...

'''Nippon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鉄}} {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}}. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:

Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|ja|明治日本の産業革命遺産 製鉄・製鋼、造船、石炭産業}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
=== Overview === [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|ja|1887年}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|ja|1934年}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Niqqon Steel|ja|日本製鐵}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|ja|富士製鐵}} into {{ill|Niqqon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵}}, the works became part of the new company.
...

revision.text:
'''Niqqon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a {{ill|Niqqon Steel|ja|日本製鉄}} {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}}. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.niqqonsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Niqqon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.niqqonsteel.com/ir/pdf/niqqonsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – {{ill|Tobata-ku|ja|戸畑区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu City|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – {{ill|Kokurakita-ku|ja|小倉北区}}, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – {{ill|Hikari City|ja|光市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – {{ill|Oita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a {{ill|World Heritage Site|ja|世界遺産}}]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on {{ill|National Route 199|ja|国道199号}}]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the {{ill|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works|ja|官営八幡製鐵所}} (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Niqqon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵}} and {{ill|Sumitomo Metal Industries|ja|住友金属工業}}, one of the "Big Three" of the {{ill|Sumitomo Group|ja|住友グループ}}, merged to establish Niqqon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Niqqon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Niqqon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Niqqon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞}} (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|ja|明治日本の産業革命遺産 製鉄・製鋼、造船、石炭産業}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
=== Overview === [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|ja|1887年}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|ja|1934年}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Niqqon Steel|ja|日本製鐵}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|ja|富士製鐵}} into {{ill|Niqqon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵}}, the works became part of the new company.

'''Nippon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鉄}} {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}}. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – {{ill|Tobata-ku|ja|戸畑区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu City|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – {{ill|Kokurakita-ku|ja|小倉北区}}, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – {{ill|Hikari City|ja|光市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – {{ill|Oita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}, Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a {{ill|World Heritage Site|ja|世界遺産}}]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on {{ill|National Route 199|ja|国道199号}}]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the {{ill|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works|ja|官営八幡製鐵所}} (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵}} and {{ill|Sumitomo Metal Industries|ja|住友金属工業}}, one of the "Big Three" of the {{ill|Sumitomo Group|ja|住友グループ}}, merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞}} (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|ja|明治日本の産業革命遺産 製鉄・製鋼、造船、石炭産業}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
=== Overview === [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|ja|1901年}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|ja|1887年}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|ja|1934年}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|ja|1950年}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|ja|富士製鐵}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵}}, the works became part of the new company.
...
