The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – {{ill|Tobata-ku|ja|戸畑区|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu City|ja|北九州市|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県|qq=draft}}, Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – {{ill|Kokurakita-ku|ja|小倉北区|qq=draft}}, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – {{ill|Hikari City|ja|光市|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県|qq=draft}}, Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – {{ill|Oita City|ja|大分市|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県|qq=draft}}, Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a {{ill|World Heritage Site|ja|世界遺産|qq=draft}}]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on {{ill|National Route 199|ja|国道199号|qq=draft}}]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港|qq=draft}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the {{ill|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works|ja|官営八幡製鐵所|qq=draft}} (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Sumitomo Metal Industries|ja|住友金属工業|qq=draft}}, one of the "Big Three" of the {{ill|Sumitomo Group|ja|住友グループ|qq=draft}}, merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所|qq=draft}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞|qq=draft}} (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室|qq=draft}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|ja|明治日本の産業革命遺産 製鉄・製鋼、造船、石炭産業}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
== Overview == [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|ja|1901st year|qq=draft}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所|qq=draft}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|ja|1901st year|qq=draft}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|qq=draft}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所|qq=draft}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|ja|1887th year|qq=draft}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦|qq=draft}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|ja|1934th year|qq=draft}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵|qq=draft}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|ja|1950th year|qq=draft}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵|qq=draft}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|ja|富士製鐵|qq=draft}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft}}, the works became part of the new company.
...

The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – {{ill|Tobata-ku|ja|戸畑区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobata-ku|qq=}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu City|ja|北九州市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu City|qq=}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuoka Prefecture|qq=}}, Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – {{ill|Kokurakita-ku|ja|小倉北区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokurakita-ku|qq=}}, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – {{ill|Hikari City|ja|光市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikari City|qq=}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Prefecture|qq=}}, Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – {{ill|Oita City|ja|大分市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita City|qq=}}, {{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Prefecture|qq=}}, Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a {{ill|World Heritage Site|ja|世界遺産|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World Heritage Site|qq=}}]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on {{ill|National Route 199|ja|国道199号|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Route 199|qq=}}]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naimin Port|qq=}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the {{ill|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works|ja|官営八幡製鐵所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Government-operated Yahata Steel Works|qq=}} (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} and {{ill|Sumitomo Metal Industries|ja|住友金属工業|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumitomo Metal Industries|qq=}}, one of the "Big Three" of the {{ill|Sumitomo Group|ja|住友グループ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumitomo Group|qq=}}, merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|qq=}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mainichi Shimbun|qq=}} (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onga River Pump Room|qq=}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|ja|明治日本の産業革命遺産 製鉄・製鋼、造船、石炭産業|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|qq=}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
== Overview == [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|ja|1901st year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1901|qq=}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/steelworks|qq=}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|ja|1901st year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1901|qq=}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|2=ja|3=明治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|qq=}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|ja|1887th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1887|qq=}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War II|qq=}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|ja|1934th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1934|qq=}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|ja|1950th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1950|qq=}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yawata Iron & Steel|qq=}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|ja|富士製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuji Iron & Steel|qq=}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel Corporation|qq=}}, the works became part of the new company.
...

After World War II, in {{ill|1950|ja|1950th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1950|qq=}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yawata Iron & Steel|qq=}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|ja|富士製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuji Iron & Steel|qq=}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel Corporation|qq=}}, the works became part of the new company.

revision.comment: Bot: remove sole member from [[Category:Nippon Steel Group]]
revision.text:
'''Nippon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鉄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/steelworks|qq=}}. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahata Works|qq=}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Works|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikari Titanium Division|qq=}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – {{ill|Tobata-ku|ja|戸畑区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobata-ku|qq=}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu City|ja|北九州市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu City|qq=}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuoka Prefecture|qq=}}, Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – {{ill|Kokurakita-ku|ja|小倉北区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokurakita-ku|qq=}}, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – {{ill|Hikari City|ja|光市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikari City|qq=}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Prefecture|qq=}}, Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – {{ill|Oita City|ja|大分市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita City|qq=}}, {{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Prefecture|qq=}}, Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a {{ill|World Heritage Site|ja|世界遺産|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World Heritage Site|qq=}}]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on {{ill|National Route 199|ja|国道199号|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Route 199|qq=}}]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naimin Port|qq=}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the {{ill|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works|ja|官営八幡製鐵所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Government-operated Yahata Steel Works|qq=}} (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} and {{ill|Sumitomo Metal Industries|ja|住友金属工業|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumitomo Metal Industries|qq=}}, one of the "Big Three" of the {{ill|Sumitomo Group|ja|住友グループ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumitomo Group|qq=}}, merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|qq=}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mainichi Shimbun|qq=}} (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onga River Pump Room|qq=}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|ja|明治日本の産業革命遺産 製鉄・製鋼、造船、石炭産業|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|qq=}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
== Overview == [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|ja|1901st year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1901|qq=}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/steelworks|qq=}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|ja|1901st year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1901|qq=}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|2=ja|3=明治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|qq=}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|ja|1887th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1887|qq=}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War II|qq=}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|ja|1934th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1934|qq=}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|ja|1950th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1950|qq=}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yawata Iron & Steel|qq=}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|ja|富士製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuji Iron & Steel|qq=}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel Corporation|qq=}}, the works became part of the new company.

[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a {{ill|World Heritage Site|ja|世界遺産|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World Heritage Site|qq=}}]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on {{ill|National Route 199|ja|国道199号|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Route 199|qq=}}]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naimin Port|qq=}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the {{ill|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works|ja|官営八幡製鐵所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Government-operated Yahata Steel Works|qq=}} (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} and {{ill|Sumitomo Metal Industries|ja|住友金属工業|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumitomo Metal Industries|qq=}}, one of the "Big Three" of the {{ill|Sumitomo Group|ja|住友グループ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumitomo Group|qq=}}, merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|qq=}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mainichi Shimbun|qq=}} (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onga River Pump Room|qq=}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|ja|明治日本の産業革命遺産 製鉄・製鋼、造船、石炭産業|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|qq=}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
== Overview == [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|ja|1901st year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1901|qq=}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/steelworks|qq=}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|ja|1901st year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1901|qq=}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|2=ja|3=明治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|qq=}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|ja|1887th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1887|qq=}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War II|qq=}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|ja|1934th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1934|qq=}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|ja|1950th year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1950|qq=}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yawata Iron & Steel|qq=}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|ja|富士製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuji Iron & Steel|qq=}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel Corporation|qq=}}, the works became part of the new company.
...

revision.comment: Bot: remove sole member from [[Category:Tobata-ku, Kitakyushu]]
revision.text:
'''Nippon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鉄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/steelworks|qq=}}. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahata Works|qq=}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Works|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikari Titanium Division|qq=}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – {{ill|Tobata-ku|ja|戸畑区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobata-ku|qq=}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu City|ja|北九州市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu City|qq=}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuoka Prefecture|qq=}}, Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – {{ill|Kokurakita-ku|ja|小倉北区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokurakita-ku|qq=}}, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – {{ill|Hikari City|ja|光市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikari City|qq=}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Prefecture|qq=}}, Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – {{ill|Oita City|ja|大分市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita City|qq=}}, {{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Prefecture|qq=}}, Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434

revision.comment: Text replacement - "th year|" to "年|"
revision.text:
'''Nippon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鉄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/steelworks|qq=}}. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahata Works|qq=}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Works|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikari Titanium Division|qq=}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – {{ill|Tobata-ku|ja|戸畑区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobata-ku|qq=}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu City|ja|北九州市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu City|qq=}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuoka Prefecture|qq=}}, Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – {{ill|Kokurakita-ku|ja|小倉北区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokurakita-ku|qq=}}, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – {{ill|Hikari City|ja|光市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikari City|qq=}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Prefecture|qq=}}, Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – {{ill|Oita City|ja|大分市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita City|qq=}}, {{ill|Oita Prefecture|ja|大分県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Prefecture|qq=}}, Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a {{ill|World Heritage Site|ja|世界遺産|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World Heritage Site|qq=}}]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on {{ill|National Route 199|ja|国道199号|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Route 199|qq=}}]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naimin Port|qq=}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the {{ill|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works|ja|官営八幡製鐵所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Government-operated Yahata Steel Works|qq=}} (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} and {{ill|Sumitomo Metal Industries|ja|住友金属工業|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumitomo Metal Industries|qq=}}, one of the "Big Three" of the {{ill|Sumitomo Group|ja|住友グループ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumitomo Group|qq=}}, merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|qq=}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mainichi Shimbun|qq=}} (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onga River Pump Room|qq=}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|ja|明治日本の産業革命遺産 製鉄・製鋼、造船、石炭産業|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|qq=}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
== Overview == [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|ja|1901st year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1901|qq=}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|steelworks|ja|製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/steelworks|qq=}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|ja|1901st year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1901|qq=}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|2=ja|3=明治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|qq=}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|ja|1887年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1887|qq=}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War II|qq=}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|ja|1934年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1934|qq=}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|ja|1950年|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1950|qq=}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yawata Iron & Steel|qq=}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|ja|富士製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuji Iron & Steel|qq=}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel Corporation|qq=}}, the works became part of the new company.
...
== Related Links == *[https://www.nipponsteel.com/works/kyushu/ Nippon Steel Kyushu Works Official Website]

'''Nippon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a [[:en:Nippon Steel|Nippon Steel]] [[:en:Ironworks|steelworks]]. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahata Works|qq=}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Works|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikari Titanium Division|qq=}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – [[:en:Tobata-ku, Kitakyūshū|Tobata-ku]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu City]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – [[:en:Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|Kokurakita-ku]], Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – [[:en:Hikari, Yamaguchi|Hikari City]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – [[:en:Ōita (city)|Oita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Oita Prefecture]], Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]]]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on [[:en:Japan National Route 199|National Route 199]]]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naimin Port|qq=}}]]

On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} and [[:en:Sumitomo Metal Industries|Sumitomo Metal Industries]], one of the "Big Three" of the [[:en:Sumitomo Group|Sumitomo Group]], merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:1901年開業の施設
revision.text:
'''Nippon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a [[:en:Nippon Steel|Nippon Steel]] [[:en:Ironworks|steelworks]]. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahata Works|qq=}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Works|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikari Titanium Division|qq=}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – [[:en:Tobata-ku, Kitakyūshū|Tobata-ku]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu City]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – [[:en:Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|Kokurakita-ku]], Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – [[:en:Hikari, Yamaguchi|Hikari City]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – [[:en:Ōita (city)|Oita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Oita Prefecture]], Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]]]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on [[:en:Japan National Route 199|National Route 199]]]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naimin Port|qq=}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the [[:en:Yahata Steel Works|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works]] (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} and [[:en:Sumitomo Metal Industries|Sumitomo Metal Industries]], one of the "Big Three" of the [[:en:Sumitomo Group|Sumitomo Group]], merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|qq=}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onga River Pump Room|qq=}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the [[:en:Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining]] World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
== Overview == [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in [[:en:1901|1901]].]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated [[:en:Ironworks|steelworks]] that began operations in [[:en:1901|1901]] during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|qq=}}, which had been operating since [[:en:1887|1887]] (Meiji 20). Before [[:en:World War II|World War II]], it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In [[:en:1934|1934]] (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in [[:en:1950|1950]] (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yawata Iron & Steel|qq=}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and [[:en:Fuji Iron & Steel|Fuji Iron & Steel]] into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel Corporation|qq=}}, the works became part of the new company.
...
== Related Links == *[https://www.nipponsteel.com/works/kyushu/ Nippon Steel Kyushu Works Official Website]

Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onga River Pump Room|qq=}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the [[:en:Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining]] World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
== Overview == [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in [[:en:1901|1901]].]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated [[:en:Ironworks|steelworks]] that began operations in [[:en:1901|1901]] during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|qq=}}, which had been operating since [[:en:1887|1887]] (Meiji 20). Before [[:en:World War II|World War II]], it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In [[:en:1934|1934]] (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in [[:en:1950|1950]] (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yawata Iron & Steel|qq=}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and [[:en:Fuji Iron & Steel|Fuji Iron & Steel]] into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel Corporation|qq=}}, the works became part of the new company.

revision.text:
'''Nippon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a [[:en:Nippon Steel|Nippon Steel]] [[:en:Ironworks|steelworks]]. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahata Works|qq=}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Works|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikari Titanium Division|qq=}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – [[:en:Tobata-ku, Kitakyūshū|Tobata-ku]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu City]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – [[:en:Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|Kokurakita-ku]], Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – [[:en:Hikari, Yamaguchi|Hikari City]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – [[:en:Ōita (city)|Oita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Oita Prefecture]], Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]]]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on [[:en:Japan National Route 199|National Route 199]]]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naimin Port|qq=}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the [[:en:Yahata Steel Works|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works]] (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} and [[:en:Sumitomo Metal Industries|Sumitomo Metal Industries]], one of the "Big Three" of the [[:en:Sumitomo Group|Sumitomo Group]], merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|qq=}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onga River Pump Room|qq=}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the [[:en:Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining]] World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
== Overview == [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in [[:en:1901|1901]].]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated [[:en:Ironworks|steelworks]] that began operations in [[:en:1901|1901]] during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|qq=}}, which had been operating since [[:en:1887|1887]] (Meiji 20). Before [[:en:World War II|World War II]], it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In [[:en:1934|1934]] (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in [[:en:1950|1950]] (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yawata Iron & Steel|qq=}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and [[:en:Fuji Iron & Steel|Fuji Iron & Steel]] into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel Corporation|qq=}}, the works became part of the new company.

revision.text:
'''Nippon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a [[:en:Nippon Steel|Nippon Steel]] [[:en:Ironworks|steelworks]]. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahata Works|qq=}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Works|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikari Titanium Division|qq=}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – [[:en:Tobata-ku, Kitakyūshū|Tobata-ku]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu City]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – [[:en:Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|Kokurakita-ku]], Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – [[:en:Hikari, Yamaguchi|Hikari City]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – [[:en:Ōita (city)|Oita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Oita Prefecture]], Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]]]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on [[:en:Japan National Route 199|National Route 199]]]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naimin Port|qq=}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the [[:en:Yahata Steel Works|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works]] (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} and [[:en:Sumitomo Metal Industries|Sumitomo Metal Industries]], one of the "Big Three" of the [[:en:Sumitomo Group|Sumitomo Group]], merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|qq=}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onga River Pump Room|qq=}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the [[:en:Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining]] World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
== Overview == [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in [[:en:1901|1901]].]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated [[:en:Ironworks|steelworks]] that began operations in [[:en:1901|1901]] during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|qq=}}, which had been operating since [[:en:1887|1887]] (Meiji 20). Before [[:en:World War II|World War II]], it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In [[:en:1934|1934]] (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in [[:en:1950|1950]] (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yawata Iron & Steel|qq=}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and [[:en:Fuji Iron & Steel|Fuji Iron & Steel]] into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel Corporation|qq=}}, the works became part of the new company.
...
== Related Links == *[https://www.nipponsteel.com/works/kyushu/ Nippon Steel Kyushu Works Official Website]

revision.text:

It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahata Works|qq=}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Works|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikari Titanium Division|qq=}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – [[:en:Tobata-ku, Kitakyūshū|Tobata-ku]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu City]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – [[:en:Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|Kokurakita-ku]], Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – [[:en:Hikari, Yamaguchi|Hikari City]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – [[:en:Ōita (city)|Oita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Oita Prefecture]], Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434

On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} and [[:en:Sumitomo Metal Industries|Sumitomo Metal Industries]], one of the "Big Three" of the [[:en:Sumitomo Group|Sumitomo Group]], merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|qq=}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onga River Pump Room|qq=}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the [[:en:Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining]] World Heritage Site (23 components in total).

== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the [[:en:Yahata Steel Works|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works]] (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} and [[:en:Sumitomo Metal Industries|Sumitomo Metal Industries]], one of the "Big Three" of the [[:en:Sumitomo Group|Sumitomo Group]], merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|qq=}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onga River Pump Room|qq=}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the [[:en:Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining]] World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
== Overview == [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in [[:en:1901|1901]].]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated [[:en:Ironworks|steelworks]] that began operations in [[:en:1901|1901]] during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|qq=}}, which had been operating since [[:en:1887|1887]] (Meiji 20). Before [[:en:World War II|World War II]], it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In [[:en:1934|1934]] (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in [[:en:1950|1950]] (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yawata Iron & Steel|qq=}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and [[:en:Fuji Iron & Steel|Fuji Iron & Steel]] into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel Corporation|qq=}}, the works became part of the new company.
...

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:Iron and steel mills of Japan
revision.text:
'''Nippon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a [[:en:Nippon Steel|Nippon Steel]] [[:en:Ironworks|steelworks]]. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahata Works|qq=}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Works|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikari Titanium Division|qq=}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – [[:en:Tobata-ku, Kitakyūshū|Tobata-ku]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu City]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – [[:en:Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|Kokurakita-ku]], Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – [[:en:Hikari, Yamaguchi|Hikari City]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – [[:en:Ōita (city)|Oita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Oita Prefecture]], Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]]]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on [[:en:Japan National Route 199|National Route 199]]]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naimin Port|qq=}}]]

On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} and [[:en:Sumitomo Metal Industries|Sumitomo Metal Industries]], one of the "Big Three" of the [[:en:Sumitomo Group|Sumitomo Group]], merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|qq=}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onga River Pump Room|qq=}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the [[:en:Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining]] World Heritage Site (23 components in total).

With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and [[:en:Fuji Iron & Steel|Fuji Iron & Steel]] into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel Corporation|qq=}}, the works became part of the new company.

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:明治日本の産業革命遺産
revision.text:
'''Nippon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a [[:en:Nippon Steel|Nippon Steel]] [[:en:Ironworks|steelworks]]. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahata Works|qq=}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Works|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikari Titanium Division|qq=}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – [[:en:Tobata-ku, Kitakyūshū|Tobata-ku]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu City]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – [[:en:Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|Kokurakita-ku]], Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – [[:en:Hikari, Yamaguchi|Hikari City]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – [[:en:Ōita (city)|Oita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Oita Prefecture]], Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]]]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on [[:en:Japan National Route 199|National Route 199]]]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naimin Port|qq=}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the [[:en:Yahata Steel Works|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works]] (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} and [[:en:Sumitomo Metal Industries|Sumitomo Metal Industries]], one of the "Big Three" of the [[:en:Sumitomo Group|Sumitomo Group]], merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|qq=}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onga River Pump Room|qq=}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the [[:en:Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining]] World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
== Overview == [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in [[:en:1901|1901]].]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated [[:en:Ironworks|steelworks]] that began operations in [[:en:1901|1901]] during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|qq=}}, which had been operating since [[:en:1887|1887]] (Meiji 20). Before [[:en:World War II|World War II]], it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In [[:en:1934|1934]] (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in [[:en:1950|1950]] (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yawata Iron & Steel|qq=}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and [[:en:Fuji Iron & Steel|Fuji Iron & Steel]] into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel Corporation|qq=}}, the works became part of the new company.
...

revision.text:
'''Nippon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a [[:en:Nippon Steel|Nippon Steel]] [[:en:Ironworks|steelworks]]. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahata Works|qq=}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Works|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikari Titanium Division|qq=}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – [[:en:Tobata-ku, Kitakyūshū|Tobata-ku]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu City]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – [[:en:Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|Kokurakita-ku]], Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – [[:en:Hikari, Yamaguchi|Hikari City]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – [[:en:Ōita (city)|Oita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Oita Prefecture]], Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]]]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on [[:en:Japan National Route 199|National Route 199]]]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naimin Port|qq=}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the [[:en:Yahata Steel Works|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works]] (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} and [[:en:Sumitomo Metal Industries|Sumitomo Metal Industries]], one of the "Big Three" of the [[:en:Sumitomo Group|Sumitomo Group]], merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|qq=}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onga River Pump Room|qq=}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the [[:en:Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining]] World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
== Overview == [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in [[:en:1901|1901]].]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated [[:en:Ironworks|steelworks]] that began operations in [[:en:1901|1901]] during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|qq=}}, which had been operating since [[:en:1887|1887]] (Meiji 20). Before [[:en:World War II|World War II]], it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In [[:en:1934|1934]] (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in [[:en:1950|1950]] (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yawata Iron & Steel|qq=}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and [[:en:Fuji Iron & Steel|Fuji Iron & Steel]] into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel Corporation|qq=}}, the works became part of the new company.
...
== Related Links == *[https://www.nipponsteel.com/works/kyushu/ Nippon Steel Kyushu Works Official Website]

* Yahata District (Tobata) – [[:en:Tobata-ku, Kitakyūshū|Tobata-ku]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu City]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – [[:en:Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|Kokurakita-ku]], Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – [[:en:Hikari, Yamaguchi|Hikari City]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – [[:en:Ōita (city)|Oita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Oita Prefecture]], Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]]]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on [[:en:Japan National Route 199|National Route 199]]]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naimin Port|qq=}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the [[:en:Yahata Steel Works|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works]] (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} and [[:en:Sumitomo Metal Industries|Sumitomo Metal Industries]], one of the "Big Three" of the [[:en:Sumitomo Group|Sumitomo Group]], merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|qq=}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onga River Pump Room|qq=}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the [[:en:Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining]] World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
== Overview == [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in [[:en:1901|1901]].]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated [[:en:Ironworks|steelworks]] that began operations in [[:en:1901|1901]] during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|qq=}}, which had been operating since [[:en:1887|1887]] (Meiji 20). Before [[:en:World War II|World War II]], it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In [[:en:1934|1934]] (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|qq=}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in [[:en:1950|1950]] (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yawata Iron & Steel|qq=}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and [[:en:Fuji Iron & Steel|Fuji Iron & Steel]] into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel Corporation|qq=}}, the works became part of the new company.
...
