revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:日本製鉄九州製鉄所]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q97311572]] Wikidata name: Nippon Steel Kyushu Works [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Nippon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a [[:en:Nippon Steel|Nippon Steel]] [[:en:Ironworks|steelworks]]. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahata Works|1=Yawata Steel Works|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97379379}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Works|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本製鉄九州製鉄所|1=Nippon Steel Kyushu Works|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97311572}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikari Titanium Division|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – [[:en:Tobata-ku, Kitakyūshū|Tobata-ku]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu City]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – [[:en:Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|Kokurakita-ku]], Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – [[:en:Hikari, Yamaguchi|Hikari City]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – [[:en:Ōita (city)|Oita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Oita Prefecture]], Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]]]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on [[:en:Japan National Route 199|National Route 199]]]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naimin Port|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the [[:en:Yahata Steel Works|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works]] (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|1=Nippon Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q651282}} and [[:en:Sumitomo Metal Industries|Sumitomo Metal Industries]], one of the "Big Three" of the [[:en:Sumitomo Group|Sumitomo Group]], merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onga River Pump Room|1=Onga River Pumping Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q58889640}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the [[:en:Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining]] World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
== Overview == [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in [[:en:1901|1901]].]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated [[:en:Ironworks|steelworks]] that began operations in [[:en:1901|1901]] during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|1=Tanaka iron works, Kamaishi mine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648139}}, which had been operating since [[:en:1887|1887]] (Meiji 20). Before [[:en:World War II|World War II]], it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In [[:en:1934|1934]] (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|1=Japan Iron & Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509078}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in [[:en:1950|1950]] (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yawata Iron & Steel|1=Yawata Iron & Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391104}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and [[:en:Fuji Iron & Steel|Fuji Iron & Steel]] into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel Corporation|1=Nippon Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q651282}}, the works became part of the new company.
...
== Related Links == *[https://www.nipponsteel.com/works/kyushu/ Nippon Steel Kyushu Works Official Website]

revision.comment: Bot: add interwikis from Wikidata
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:日本製鉄九州製鉄所]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:日本製鉄九州製鉄所]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:日本製鉄九州製鉄所]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q97311572]] Wikidata name: Nippon Steel Kyushu Works [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Nippon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a [[:en:Nippon Steel|Nippon Steel]] [[:en:Ironworks|steelworks]]. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahata Works|1=Yawata Steel Works|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97379379}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Works|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本製鉄九州製鉄所|1=Nippon Steel Kyushu Works|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97311572}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikari Titanium Division|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – [[:en:Tobata-ku, Kitakyūshū|Tobata-ku]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu City]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – [[:en:Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|Kokurakita-ku]], Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – [[:en:Hikari, Yamaguchi|Hikari City]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – [[:en:Ōita (city)|Oita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Oita Prefecture]], Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]]]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on [[:en:Japan National Route 199|National Route 199]]]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naimin Port|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the [[:en:Yahata Steel Works|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works]] (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|1=Nippon Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q651282}} and [[:en:Sumitomo Metal Industries|Sumitomo Metal Industries]], one of the "Big Three" of the [[:en:Sumitomo Group|Sumitomo Group]], merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onga River Pump Room|1=Onga River Pumping Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q58889640}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the [[:en:Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining]] World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
== Overview == [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in [[:en:1901|1901]].]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated [[:en:Ironworks|steelworks]] that began operations in [[:en:1901|1901]] during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|1=Tanaka iron works, Kamaishi mine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648139}}, which had been operating since [[:en:1887|1887]] (Meiji 20). Before [[:en:World War II|World War II]], it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In [[:en:1934|1934]] (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|1=Japan Iron & Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509078}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in [[:en:1950|1950]] (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yawata Iron & Steel|1=Yawata Iron & Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391104}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and [[:en:Fuji Iron & Steel|Fuji Iron & Steel]] into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel Corporation|1=Nippon Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q651282}}, the works became part of the new company.
...

After World War II, in [[:en:1950|1950]] (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yawata Iron & Steel|1=Yawata Iron & Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391104}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and [[:en:Fuji Iron & Steel|Fuji Iron & Steel]] into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel Corporation|1=Nippon Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q651282}}, the works became part of the new company.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "↵↵↵↵↵" to " "
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:日本製鉄九州製鉄所]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:日本製鉄九州製鉄所]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:日本製鉄九州製鉄所]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q97311572]] Wikidata name: Nippon Steel Kyushu Works [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Nippon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a [[:en:Nippon Steel|Nippon Steel]] [[:en:Ironworks|steelworks]]. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahata Works|1=Yawata Steel Works|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97379379}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Works|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本製鉄九州製鉄所|1=Nippon Steel Kyushu Works|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97311572}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikari Titanium Division|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – [[:en:Tobata-ku, Kitakyūshū|Tobata-ku]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu City]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – [[:en:Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|Kokurakita-ku]], Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – [[:en:Hikari, Yamaguchi|Hikari City]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – [[:en:Ōita (city)|Oita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Oita Prefecture]], Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]]]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on [[:en:Japan National Route 199|National Route 199]]]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naimin Port|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the [[:en:Yahata Steel Works|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works]] (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|1=Nippon Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q651282}} and [[:en:Sumitomo Metal Industries|Sumitomo Metal Industries]], one of the "Big Three" of the [[:en:Sumitomo Group|Sumitomo Group]], merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onga River Pump Room|1=Onga River Pumping Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q58889640}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the [[:en:Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining]] World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
== Overview == [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in [[:en:1901|1901]].]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated [[:en:Ironworks|steelworks]] that began operations in [[:en:1901|1901]] during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|1=Tanaka iron works, Kamaishi mine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648139}}, which had been operating since [[:en:1887|1887]] (Meiji 20). Before [[:en:World War II|World War II]], it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In [[:en:1934|1934]] (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|1=Japan Iron & Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509078}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in [[:en:1950|1950]] (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yawata Iron & Steel|1=Yawata Iron & Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391104}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and [[:en:Fuji Iron & Steel|Fuji Iron & Steel]] into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel Corporation|1=Nippon Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q651282}}, the works became part of the new company.
...

"1901" marks the start of operation in [[:en:1901|1901]].]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated [[:en:Ironworks|steelworks]] that began operations in [[:en:1901|1901]] during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|1=Tanaka iron works, Kamaishi mine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648139}}, which had been operating since [[:en:1887|1887]] (Meiji 20). Before [[:en:World War II|World War II]], it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In [[:en:1934|1934]] (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|1=Japan Iron & Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509078}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in [[:en:1950|1950]] (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yawata Iron & Steel|1=Yawata Iron & Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391104}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and [[:en:Fuji Iron & Steel|Fuji Iron & Steel]] into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel Corporation|1=Nippon Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q651282}}, the works became part of the new company.
...

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:日本製鉄九州製鉄所]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:日本製鉄九州製鉄所]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:日本製鉄九州製鉄所]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q97311572]] Wikidata name: Nippon Steel Kyushu Works [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Nippon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a [[:en:Nippon Steel|Nippon Steel]] [[:en:Ironworks|steelworks]]. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahata Works|1=Yawata Steel Works|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97379379}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Works|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本製鉄九州製鉄所|1=Nippon Steel Kyushu Works|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97311572}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikari Titanium Division|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – [[:en:Tobata-ku, Kitakyūshū|Tobata-ku]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu City]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – [[:en:Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|Kokurakita-ku]], Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – [[:en:Hikari, Yamaguchi|Hikari City]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – [[:en:Ōita (city)|Oita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Oita Prefecture]], Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Site]]]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on [[:en:Japan National Route 199|National Route 199]]]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naimin Port|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the [[:en:Yahata Steel Works|Government-operated Yahata Steel Works]] (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|1=Nippon Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q651282}} and [[:en:Sumitomo Metal Industries|Sumitomo Metal Industries]], one of the "Big Three" of the [[:en:Sumitomo Group|Sumitomo Group]], merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onga River Pump Room|1=Onga River Pumping Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q58889640}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the [[:en:Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining]] World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
== Overview == [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in [[:en:1901|1901]].]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated [[:en:Ironworks|steelworks]] that began operations in [[:en:1901|1901]] during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|1=Tanaka iron works, Kamaishi mine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648139}}, which had been operating since [[:en:1887|1887]] (Meiji 20). Before [[:en:World War II|World War II]], it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In [[:en:1934|1934]] (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|1=Japan Iron & Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509078}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in [[:en:1950|1950]] (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yawata Iron & Steel|1=Yawata Iron & Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391104}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and [[:en:Fuji Iron & Steel|Fuji Iron & Steel]] into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel Corporation|1=Nippon Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q651282}}, the works became part of the new company.
...

[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site}}]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on {{ill|Japan National Route 199|en|Japan National Route 199|WD=Q6156926|lt=National Route 199}}]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naimin Port|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]]

On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|1=Nippon Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q651282}} and {{ill|Sumitomo Metal Industries|en|Sumitomo Metal Industries|WD=Q1972867|lt=Sumitomo Metal Industries}}, one of the "Big Three" of the {{ill|Sumitomo Group|en|Sumitomo Group|WD=Q717318|lt=Sumitomo Group}}, merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onga River Pump Room|1=Onga River Pumping Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q58889640}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining|en|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining|WD=Q7751872|lt=Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).

Before {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|en|1934|WD=Q18714|lt=1934}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|1=Japan Iron & Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509078}} (Nittetsu). The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – [[:en:Tobata-ku, Kitakyūshū|Tobata-ku]], {{ill|Kitakyushu|en|Kitakyushu|WD=Q188806|lt=Kitakyushu City}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture}}, Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – {{ill|Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|en|Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|WD=Q1042291|lt=Kokurakita-ku}}, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – {{ill|Hikari, Yamaguchi|en|Hikari, Yamaguchi|WD=Q823502|lt=Hikari City}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|en|Yamaguchi Prefecture|WD=Q127264|lt=Yamaguchi Prefecture}}, Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – {{ill|Ōita (city)|en|Ōita (city)|WD=Q199609|lt=Oita City}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Oita Prefecture}}, Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site}}]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on {{ill|Japan National Route 199|en|Japan National Route 199|WD=Q6156926|lt=National Route 199}}]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naimin Port|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the {{ill|Yahata Steel Works|en|Yahata Steel Works|WD=Q2943310|lt=Government-operated Yahata Steel Works}} (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|1=Nippon Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q651282}} and {{ill|Sumitomo Metal Industries|en|Sumitomo Metal Industries|WD=Q1972867|lt=Sumitomo Metal Industries}}, one of the "Big Three" of the {{ill|Sumitomo Group|en|Sumitomo Group|WD=Q717318|lt=Sumitomo Group}}, merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|en|Mainichi Shimbun|WD=Q1136866|lt=Mainichi Shimbun}} (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onga River Pump Room|1=Onga River Pumping Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q58889640}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining|en|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining|WD=Q7751872|lt=Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
== Overview == [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|en|1901|WD=Q2035|lt=1901}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|Ironworks|en|Ironworks|WD=Q10832530|lt=steelworks}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|en|1901|WD=Q2035|lt=1901}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|1=Tanaka iron works, Kamaishi mine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648139}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|en|1887|WD=Q7826|lt=1887}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|en|1934|WD=Q18714|lt=1934}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|1=Japan Iron & Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509078}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|en|1950|WD=Q18597|lt=1950}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yawata Iron & Steel|1=Yawata Iron & Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391104}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|en|Fuji Iron & Steel|WD=Q10954021|lt=Fuji Iron & Steel}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel Corporation|1=Nippon Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q651282}}, the works became part of the new company.
...

With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|en|Fuji Iron & Steel|WD=Q10954021|lt=Fuji Iron & Steel}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel Corporation|1=Nippon Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q651282}}, the works became part of the new company.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:日本製鉄九州製鉄所]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:日本製鉄九州製鉄所]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:日本製鉄九州製鉄所]]
'''Nippon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a {{ill|Nippon Steel|en|Nippon Steel|WD=Q1151693|lt=Nippon Steel}} {{ill|Ironworks|en|Ironworks|WD=Q10832530|lt=steelworks}}. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahata Works|1=Yawata Steel Works|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97379379}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Works|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本製鉄九州製鉄所|1=Nippon Steel Kyushu Works|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97311572}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikari Titanium Division|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – [[:en:Tobata-ku, Kitakyūshū|Tobata-ku]], {{ill|Kitakyushu|en|Kitakyushu|WD=Q188806|lt=Kitakyushu City}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture}}, Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – {{ill|Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|en|Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|WD=Q1042291|lt=Kokurakita-ku}}, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – {{ill|Hikari, Yamaguchi|en|Hikari, Yamaguchi|WD=Q823502|lt=Hikari City}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|en|Yamaguchi Prefecture|WD=Q127264|lt=Yamaguchi Prefecture}}, Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – {{ill|Ōita (city)|en|Ōita (city)|WD=Q199609|lt=Oita City}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Oita Prefecture}}, Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site}}]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on {{ill|Japan National Route 199|en|Japan National Route 199|WD=Q6156926|lt=National Route 199}}]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naimin Port|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the {{ill|Yahata Steel Works|en|Yahata Steel Works|WD=Q2943310|lt=Government-operated Yahata Steel Works}} (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|1=Nippon Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q651282}} and {{ill|Sumitomo Metal Industries|en|Sumitomo Metal Industries|WD=Q1972867|lt=Sumitomo Metal Industries}}, one of the "Big Three" of the {{ill|Sumitomo Group|en|Sumitomo Group|WD=Q717318|lt=Sumitomo Group}}, merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|en|Mainichi Shimbun|WD=Q1136866|lt=Mainichi Shimbun}} (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onga River Pump Room|1=Onga River Pumping Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q58889640}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining|en|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining|WD=Q7751872|lt=Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
== Overview == [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|en|1901|WD=Q2035|lt=1901}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|Ironworks|en|Ironworks|WD=Q10832530|lt=steelworks}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|en|1901|WD=Q2035|lt=1901}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|1=Tanaka iron works, Kamaishi mine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648139}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|en|1887|WD=Q7826|lt=1887}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|en|1934|WD=Q18714|lt=1934}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|1=Japan Iron & Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509078}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|en|1950|WD=Q18597|lt=1950}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yawata Iron & Steel|1=Yawata Iron & Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391104}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|en|Fuji Iron & Steel|WD=Q10954021|lt=Fuji Iron & Steel}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel Corporation|1=Nippon Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q651282}}, the works became part of the new company.
...

The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – [[:en:Tobata-ku, Kitakyūshū|Tobata-ku]], {{ill|Kitakyushu|en|Kitakyushu|WD=Q188806|lt=Kitakyushu City|1=Kitakyushu}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}, Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – {{ill|Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|en|Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|WD=Q1042291|lt=Kokurakita-ku|1=Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū}}, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – {{ill|Hikari, Yamaguchi|en|Hikari, Yamaguchi|WD=Q823502|lt=Hikari City|1=Hikari, Yamaguchi}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|en|Yamaguchi Prefecture|WD=Q127264|lt=Yamaguchi Prefecture|1=Yamaguchi Prefecture}}, Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – {{ill|Ōita (city)|en|Ōita (city)|WD=Q199609|lt=Oita City|1=Ōita (city)}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Oita Prefecture|1=Ōita Prefecture}}, Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on {{ill|Japan National Route 199|en|Japan National Route 199|WD=Q6156926|lt=National Route 199|1=Japan National Route 199}}]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naimin Port|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the {{ill|Yahata Steel Works|en|Yahata Steel Works|WD=Q2943310|lt=Government-operated Yahata Steel Works|1=Yahata Steel Works}} (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|1=Nippon Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q651282}} and {{ill|Sumitomo Metal Industries|en|Sumitomo Metal Industries|WD=Q1972867|lt=Sumitomo Metal Industries|1=Sumitomo Metal Industries}}, one of the "Big Three" of the {{ill|Sumitomo Group|en|Sumitomo Group|WD=Q717318|lt=Sumitomo Group|1=Sumitomo Group}}, merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|en|Mainichi Shimbun|WD=Q1136866|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|1=Mainichi Shimbun}} (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onga River Pump Room|1=Onga River Pumping Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q58889640}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining|en|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining|WD=Q7751872|lt=Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|1=Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
== Overview == [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|en|1901|WD=Q2035|lt=1901|1=1901}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|Ironworks|en|Ironworks|WD=Q10832530|lt=steelworks|1=Ironworks}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|en|1901|WD=Q2035|lt=1901|1=1901}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|1=Tanaka iron works, Kamaishi mine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648139}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|en|1887|WD=Q7826|lt=1887|1=1887}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II|1=World War II}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|en|1934|WD=Q18714|lt=1934|1=1934}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|1=Japan Iron & Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509078}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|en|1950|WD=Q18597|lt=1950|1=1950}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yawata Iron & Steel|1=Yawata Iron & Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391104}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|en|Fuji Iron & Steel|WD=Q10954021|lt=Fuji Iron & Steel|1=Fuji Iron & Steel}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel Corporation|1=Nippon Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q651282}}, the works became part of the new company.
...

It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahata Works|1=Yawata Steel Works|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97379379}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Works|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本製鉄九州製鉄所|1=Nippon Steel Yawata Works|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97311572}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikari Titanium Division|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>

== Overview == [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|en|1901|WD=Q2035|lt=1901|1=1901}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|Ironworks|en|Ironworks|WD=Q10832530|lt=steelworks|1=Ironworks}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|en|1901|WD=Q2035|lt=1901|1=1901}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|1=Tanaka iron works, Kamaishi mine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648139}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|en|1887|WD=Q7826|lt=1887|1=1887}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II|1=World War II}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|en|1934|WD=Q18714|lt=1934|1=1934}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|1=Japan Iron & Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509078}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|en|1950|WD=Q18597|lt=1950|1=1950}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yawata Iron & Steel|1=Yawata Iron & Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391104}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|en|Fuji Iron & Steel|WD=Q10954021|lt=Fuji Iron & Steel|1=Fuji Iron & Steel}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel Corporation|1=Nippon Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q651282}}, the works became part of the new company.

revision.comment: Bot: strip HTML comments; consolidate 5 interlanguage link(s) into wikidata link template (has duplicate-language pairs) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/25155405060|time triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
'''Nippon Steel Kyushu Works''' (日本製鉄九州製鉄所) is a {{ill|Nippon Steel|en|Nippon Steel|WD=Q1151693|lt=Nippon Steel|1=Nippon Steel}} {{ill|Ironworks|en|Ironworks|WD=Q10832530|lt=steelworks|1=Ironworks}}. It was established on April 1, 2020, through the integration of the {{ill|Yahata Works|ja|八幡製鉄所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahata Works|1=Yawata Steel Works|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97379379}}, {{ill|Oita Works|ja|大分製鉄所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oita Works|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本製鉄九州製鉄所|1=Nippon Steel Yawata Works|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97311572}}, and {{ill|Hikari Titanium Division|ja|光チタン部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikari Titanium Division|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="saihen">{{cite news|title=On the Integration and Reorganization of Steelworks|url=https://www.nipponsteel.com/common/secure/news/20191101_100.pdf|publisher=Nippon Steel|date=2019-11-01|accessdate=2020-01-31}}</ref> As of March 31, 2021, the number of employees is 5,666.<ref name="kessann2021">[https://www.nipponsteel.com/ir/pdf/nipponsteel_jp_br_2020_all.pdf 2020 Financial Report]</ref>
The works are divided into the following areas: Yahata District (Tobata), Yahata District (Kokura), Yahata District (Hikari Titanium), Oita District (Oita), and Oita District (Hikari Pipe). The respective locations are:
* Yahata District (Tobata) – [[:en:Tobata-ku, Kitakyūshū|Tobata-ku]], {{ill|Kitakyushu|en|Kitakyushu|WD=Q188806|lt=Kitakyushu City|1=Kitakyushu}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}, Tobata-cho 1-1 * Yahata District (Kokura) – {{ill|Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|en|Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū|WD=Q1042291|lt=Kokurakita-ku|1=Kokurakita-ku, Kitakyūshū}}, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture, Konemicho 1 * Yahata District (Hikari Titanium) – {{ill|Hikari, Yamaguchi|en|Hikari, Yamaguchi|WD=Q823502|lt=Hikari City|1=Hikari, Yamaguchi}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|en|Yamaguchi Prefecture|WD=Q127264|lt=Yamaguchi Prefecture|1=Yamaguchi Prefecture}}, Shimada 3434 * Oita District (Oita) – {{ill|Ōita (city)|en|Ōita (city)|WD=Q199609|lt=Oita City|1=Ōita (city)}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Oita Prefecture|1=Ōita Prefecture}}, Nishinosu 1 * Oita District (Hikari Pipe) – Hikari City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Shimada 3434
[[File:140721 Yawata Steel Works from RRH Kitakyushu Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|right|Yawata District]] [[File:八幡製鉄所旧本事務所.JPG|thumb|Former Main Office, a {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}]] [[File:Murasakigawa-river branching bay from Murasakigawa-bigbridge 1.jpg|thumb|right|Kokura District (background) as seen from the Murakami River Bridge on {{ill|Japan National Route 199|en|Japan National Route 199|WD=Q6156926|lt=National Route 199|1=Japan National Route 199}}]] [[File:Sumitomo Metal Industries Kokura, -4 Aug. 2011 a.jpg|thumb|right|View from the {{ill|Naimin Port|ja|日明港|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naimin Port|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]]
== Yahata District == The Yahata District has a history originating from the {{ill|Yahata Steel Works|en|Yahata Steel Works|WD=Q2943310|lt=Government-operated Yahata Steel Works|1=Yahata Steel Works}} (Yahatahigashi-ku, Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture). On October 1, 2012, {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|新日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|1=Nippon Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q651282}} and {{ill|Sumitomo Metal Industries|en|Sumitomo Metal Industries|WD=Q1972867|lt=Sumitomo Metal Industries|1=Sumitomo Metal Industries}}, one of the "Big Three" of the {{ill|Sumitomo Group|en|Sumitomo Group|WD=Q717318|lt=Sumitomo Group|1=Sumitomo Group}}, merged to establish Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal (renamed Nippon Steel in April 2019). Kokura District traces its roots back to the {{ill|Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja|東京製鋼株式会社小倉製鋼所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Steel Co., Ltd. Kokura Steel Plant|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which was completed in 1916. Before the integration, it was known as Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal Kokura Steelworks. On April 1, 2014, it became part of Yahata Works.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-4-1">Munekazu Ishida (April 1, 2014). "Nippon Steel & Sumitomo Metal: New 'Yawata' is born today—Integration of two steelworks, 'Kokura' no longer remains." {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|en|Mainichi Shimbun|WD=Q1136866|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|1=Mainichi Shimbun}} (Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
Among the assets of the Yahata Steelworks, the Former Main Office, Repair Shop, Former Forging Shop (Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture), and the {{ill|Onga River Pump Room|ja|遠賀川水源地ポンプ室|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onga River Pump Room|1=Onga River Pumping Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q58889640}} (Nakama City, Fukuoka Prefecture) are registered as components of the {{ill|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining|en|Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining|WD=Q7751872|lt=Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding, and Coal Mining|1=Sites of Japan's Meiji Industrial Revolution: Iron and Steel, Shipbuilding and Coal Mining}} World Heritage Site (23 components in total).
== Overview == [[File:Higashida First Blast Furnace.JPG|thumb|right|Site of the Higashida First Blast Furnace. "1901" marks the start of operation in {{ill|1901|en|1901|WD=Q2035|lt=1901|1=1901}}.]] The Yahata District originated from a government-operated {{ill|Ironworks|en|Ironworks|WD=Q10832530|lt=steelworks|1=Ironworks}} that began operations in {{ill|1901|en|1901|WD=Q2035|lt=1901|1=1901}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}. It was Japan's second steelworks, following the {{ill|Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|ja|釜石鉱山田中製鉄所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamaishi Mine Tanaka Steelworks|1=Tanaka iron works, Kamaishi mine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648139}}, which had been operating since {{ill|1887|en|1887|WD=Q7826|lt=1887|1=1887}} (Meiji 20). Before {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|WD=Q362|lt=World War II|1=World War II}}, it was the leading domestic steelworks, producing more than half of Japan's steel. In {{ill|1934|en|1934|WD=Q18714|lt=1934|1=1934}} (Showa 9), the government-operated steelworks merged with private companies to establish {{ill|Nippon Steel|ja|日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel|1=Japan Iron & Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509078}} (Nittetsu). After World War II, in {{ill|1950|en|1950|WD=Q18597|lt=1950|1=1950}} (Showa 25), Nittetsu was dismantled, and Yahata Steelworks became part of {{ill|Yawata Iron & Steel|ja|八幡製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yawata Iron & Steel|1=Yawata Iron & Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11391104}}. With the 1970 (Showa 45) merger of Yawata Iron & Steel and {{ill|Fuji Iron & Steel|en|Fuji Iron & Steel|WD=Q10954021|lt=Fuji Iron & Steel|1=Fuji Iron & Steel}} into {{ill|Nippon Steel Corporation|ja|新日本製鐵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippon Steel Corporation|1=Nippon Steel|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q651282}}, the works became part of the new company.
